claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me about each of them and their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex is 8 years old, so definitely under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Alex, at 8 years old, is definitely under 16, which means they automatically qualify as a "qualifying child" based on age. That's a great start!

Let me move to the next step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work status.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

At 8 years old, I would expect Alex isn't working, but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment. A simple yes or no will do!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Of course! That makes perfect sense - Alex is just 8 years old. So that's a clear "yes" - Alex is working less than 24 hours per week (in fact, not working at all).

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, this refers to benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

At 8 years old, Alex almost certainly wouldn't be receiving these benefits in his own name, but I just need to confirm. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about apprenticeships.

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

At 8 years old, I'm confident the answer is no, but just to confirm - Alex is not enrolled in any apprenticeship programme, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's just in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right - primary school at 8 years old! So Alex is not in an apprenticeship. Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and meet additional criteria (such as working, seeking work, studying, or having sufficient resources)
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad

The most common scenario is simply that you normally live in the UK. 

**Do you normally live in the UK?** (Or does one of the other options apply to you?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you normally live in the UK, so you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** - this is the most common situation
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex lives with you, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Please let me know if:
- **No one else is claiming** for Alex
- **Someone else was claiming, but you've agreed that you will now be the claimant**
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to understand Alex's current living situation.

**Where does Alex currently live?**

- **At home with you** (normal living situation)
- **In hospital**
- **In local authority care**
- **With foster parents**

I assume Alex is living at home with you in Manchester, but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex has been in hospital for a while now - a few months.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. I hope he's getting the care he needs.

I need to check on the duration of his hospital stay, as this can affect eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. This is important information.

**How long has Alex been in hospital in total?**

The rules are:
- Child Benefit normally continues for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If there were multiple hospital stays with less than 28 days between them, those stays are added together

Could you tell me approximately how long Alex has been in hospital? You mentioned "a few months" - is this:
- **Less than 12 weeks** (under about 3 months)?
- **More than 12 weeks** (over about 3 months)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 11 weeks now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. At 11 weeks, Alex is still under the 12-week limit, which is good news for your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I have a question that's relevant both now and if Alex's stay extends beyond 12 weeks.

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Bringing him food, snacks, or treats
- Buying clothes, toys, or books
- Paying for entertainment (games, magazines, etc.)
- Any other regular expenses for his care or comfort

If you have a partner (spouse or civil partner) you live with, their spending counts too.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy his clothes, magazines, and his favorite food to bring when I visit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's lovely that you're visiting and supporting Alex in that way. Since you're regularly spending money on him, this is important - it means you can continue to receive Child Benefit even if his hospital stay extends beyond 12 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's hospital stay being funded through the NHS (as is typical), rather than the local council paying for his accommodation or care costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's just NHS hospital care - the council isn't paying anything.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment. Let me now provide you with the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary of Your Situation:

| Requirement | Status |
|-------------|--------|
| Alex is under 16 | ✅ (8 years old) |
| You live in the UK | ✅ (Manchester) |
| You're responsible for Alex | ✅ (he lives with you) |
| No other claimants | ✅ |
| Hospital stay | ✅ (11 weeks - under the 12-week limit) |
| Regularly spending on Alex | ✅ (clothes, magazines, food) |
| Council not paying for care | ✅ (NHS hospital care) |

### Important Note About Alex's Hospital Stay:
Since you're regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital, you'll be able to continue receiving Child Benefit even if his stay extends beyond 12 weeks. I hope he recovers soon! 💙

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate)
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or a partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax

📎 **More information and how to claim:** [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex qualifies as a 'qualifying child' because: (1) He is under 16 years old (8 years old), (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week, (3) He does not receive benefits in his own right, (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant qualifies because: (1) They normally live in the UK (Manchester), (2) They are responsible for Alex as he lives with them, (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Regarding Alex's hospital stay: (1) He has been in hospital for 11 weeks, which is under the 12-week limit, (2) The claimant regularly spends money on Alex (clothes, magazines, food), which means eligibility would continue even beyond 12 weeks, (3) The local council is not paying for his accommodation - it's NHS hospital care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. All eligibility criteria are met: the child is under 16, the claimant lives in the UK, they are responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming, and although Alex is currently in hospital (11 weeks), this is within the 12-week limit and the claimant continues to spend money on him regularly, which would protect eligibility even if the stay extends beyond 12 weeks.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex qualifies as a 'qualifying child' because: (1) He is under 16 years old (8 years old), (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week, (3) He does not receive benefits in his own right, (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant qualifies because: (1) They normally live in the UK (Manchester), (2) They are responsible for Alex as he lives with them, (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Regarding Alex's hospital stay: (1) He has been in hospital for 11 weeks, which is under the 12-week limit, (2) The claimant regularly spends money on Alex (clothes, magazines, food), which means eligibility would continue even beyond 12 weeks, (3) The local council is not paying for his accommodation - it's NHS hospital care.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. All eligibility criteria are met: the child is under 16, the claimant lives in the UK, they are responsible for Alex, no one else is claiming, and although Alex is currently in hospital (11 weeks), this is within the 12-week limit and the claimant continues to spend money on him regularly, which would protect eligibility even if the stay extends beyond 12 weeks.
